sections.content: A Personal Locator Beacon (PLB) is a compact, portable 406 MHz distress beacon registered to an individual person rather than a vessel. PLBs operate through the same Cospas-Sarsat satellite system as EPIRBs but are smaller, lighter, and designed for personal use. They are manually activated and intended as a last-resort distress signal when a person is separated from the vessel or when ship-based equipment is unavailable.

While PLBs provide a valuable personal safety net, they are not a substitute for the vessel's EPIRB. The shorter battery life (24+ hours versus 48+ hours for an EPIRB) and the lack of automatic float-free activation mean that PLBs serve a complementary role in the overall safety equipment inventory.

sections.title: GPS-DSC Integration
sections.content: When a GPS receiver is connected to the VHF DSC radio, the vessel's position and time are automatically included in every DSC alert. This GPS-DSC integration is critical for distress alerting — if the GPS feed fails, the operator must enter the position manually before sending a DSC distress alert.

Accurate position reporting is fundamental to maritime safety and SAR operations. The standard maritime position format uses latitude expressed as degrees and decimal minutes (DD° MM.M' N/S) and longitude as degrees and decimal minutes (DDD° MM.M' E/W). For example, a position might be written as 51° 28.3' N, 003° 12.7' W.

When communicating position by voice radio, the standard phraseology is: 'My position is [latitude] North/South, [longitude] East/West.' For example: 'My position is five one degrees two eight decimal three minutes North, zero zero three degrees one two decimal seven minutes West.' Alternatively, position may be given as a bearing and distance from a known landmark: 'My position is two four five degrees from Cape Clear lighthouse, distance one two nautical miles.'

sections.title: GPS Failure
sections.content: If the GPS connection to the DSC radio is lost, the radio may display a 'no position' warning. The operator must manually enter the vessel's last known or estimated position before transmitting a DSC distress alert. Sending a distress alert without position data severely hampers the SAR response.

sections.prompt: What is the minimum battery life required for a PLB?
sections.explanation: PLBs must provide at least 24 hours of continuous transmission. This is shorter than the 48-hour requirement for EPIRBs, reflecting the PLB's role as a compact personal device rather than a primary vessel distress beacon.

sections.prompt: Maritime positions use the format: latitude DD° MM.M' N/S, longitude ___ E/W.
sections.explanation: Longitude is expressed in degrees (three digits, 000-180), decimal minutes, and East or West hemisphere indicator. The three-digit format for longitude degrees distinguishes it from the two-digit latitude format.

sections.prompt: If the GPS feed to your DSC radio fails, what must you do before sending a distress alert?

options.text: Send the alert without position — the satellite will locate you
Wait until the GPS reconnects before sending any alert
Manually enter your last known or estimated position into the DSC radio

sections.explanation: If the GPS feed is lost, the operator must manually enter the vessel's position before transmitting a DSC distress alert. Sending a distress alert without position data makes it far harder for SAR services to locate you. Do not wait for GPS to reconnect if the situation is urgent.

points: PLBs are personal 406 MHz beacons registered to an individual, with 24+ hour battery life and manual activation only.
PLBs complement but do not replace the vessel's EPIRB.
GPS-DSC integration automatically includes position and time in DSC alerts; if GPS fails, enter position manually.
Standard maritime position format: latitude DD° MM.M' N/S, longitude DDD° MM.M' E/W.
Position can also be communicated as a bearing and distance from a known landmark.
